content.problemStatement:
Teddy and Tracy like to play a game based on strings. The game is as follows. Initially, Tracy writes a long random string on a whiteboard. Then, each player starting with Teddy makes turn alternately. Each turn, the player must erase a contiguous substring that exists in the dictionary. The dictionary consists of N words.

Of course, the player that can't erase any substring in his turn loses the game, and the other player is declared the winner.

Note that after a substring R is erased, the remaining substring becomes separated, i.e. they cannot erase a word that occurs partially to the left of R and partially to the right of R.


Determine the winner of the game, assuming that both players play optimally.
content.inputFormat:
The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case:
- A line with the string S (1 ≤ |S| ≤ 30), consisting of lowercase letters.
- A line with an integer N (1 ≤ N ≤ 30), the size of the dictionary.
- N following lines, each containing a word wi (1 ≤ |wi| ≤ 30), also lowercase.
All strings contain no spaces.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the name of the winner when both players play optimally: either "Teddy" (the first player) or "Tracy" (the second player).

sampleTestCases.input:
3
codechef
2
code
chef
foo
1
bar
mississippi
4
ssissi
mippi
mi
ppi
sampleTestCases.explanation: In the first test case, Teddy can remove 'code' or 'chef' and win. In the second test case, Tracy can remove 'bar' and win. In the third test case, Teddy can remove 'ssissi' and win.

solution.approach: The game is impartial: after erasing a dictionary word the remaining left and right parts become independent subgames. For any substring S[l..r] we define Grundy(l,r) as the mex of Grundy(l,k‑1) xor Grundy(k+len, r) over every occurrence of a dictionary word of length len that starts at position k (l ≤ k ≤ r‑len+1). An empty substring has Grundy 0. The whole string is a winning position for the first player iff Grundy(0,|S|‑1) ≠ 0. Because |S| ≤ 30 we can compute Grundy for all O(|S|^2) intervals using memoised recursion or bottom‑up DP. For each interval we collect reachable Grundy values in a small boolean array (size ≤ 64 is enough) and take the minimum excluded value. The overall complexity per test case is O(|S|^3·N) ≤ 30^3·30 ≈ 8·10^4 operations, easily fast. Memory usage is O(|S|^2).
solution.timeComplexity: O(|S|^3 · N) per test case (worst‑case about 8·10^4 operations for the given limits).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(|S|^2) for the DP table (≤ 900 integers).
